name: The Great Gas Giveaway
content:
ACT Independent Senator David Pocock is today tabling a motion proposing to establish a special senate committee to inquire into the extraordinarily low rates of revenue Australians receive from the export of our gas resources.

Proposed as the “Select Committee on Why Gas Companies Pay Less for Offshore Liquid Natural Gas than Australians Pay in Beer Excise”, it would examine the amount of Petroleum Resource Rent Tax (PRRT) paid on Liquified Natural Gas (LNG) and why it is so low, comparable policies in other jurisdictions like Norway and Qatar and the Australian Council of Trade Union’s proposal for a 25% tax on gas export revenue.

The inquiry would also examine the impact on Australian businesses and households of the increase in gas prices since 2016 and what could be done with the additional revenue generated by effectively taxing the offshore LNG industry.

It would comprise 2 Labor, 2 Coalition and one crossbench senator alongside Senator Pocock as Chair and report in May 2026.

The proposal will be put to a vote in the Senate tomorrow.

Senator Pocock said Australians have had enough of multinational gas companies profiting off our resources without providing a fair return.

“We get one chance to capture the benefits of the LNG boom and invest in the things Australians need most: housing, health, education,” Senator Pocock said.

“Currently we are squandering what Norway has turned into a $3 trillion dollar sovereign wealth fund.

“Governments of all political persuasions are constantly telling us budgets are about priorities and asking for solutions, this proposal ticks both those boxes.

“I call on the major parties to stand up for what the people they’ve been elected to represent want to see and that’s big companies paying more to export our gas than Australians pay on beer excise.”

name: International Long COVID Awareness Week 2026
content:
The Campaign:
Organised by Emerge Australia, International Long COVID Awareness Week runs from Monday, 9 March to Sunday, 15 March 2026, under the theme "Breaking Barriers, Building Support." The week aims to raise critical awareness about the growing needs of Australians living with Long COVID and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS). Key events include the release of a 7-part "Imagine" podcast series featuring medical experts and patients, the Emerge Australia Awards for Journalism Excellence, and a crucial Parliamentary Friends of ME/CFS meeting on 10 March, bringing together parliamentarians, clinicians, and patients to advocate for stronger policy outcomes and coordinated research investment.

The Impact on Australia's Economy and Workforce:
Long COVID is a mass-disabling event that has dealt a severe blow to the Australian workforce. A joint study by the University of Melbourne, ANU, and UNSW found that Long COVID cost the Australian economy approximately $9.6 billion in lost productivity in 2022 alone—equivalent to a quarter of Australia’s real GDP growth that year. Workers experiencing ongoing symptoms resulted in roughly 100 million lost labour hours. The economic burden is disproportionately falling on the prime working-age demographic, with 30- to 49-year-olds accounting for more than 50% of the lost productivity. At its peak, up to 1.3 million Australians were living with Long COVID, and hundreds of thousands remain severely affected 12 months or more after their initial infection.

The Underfunding and Health System Crisis:
Despite the staggering economic and human cost, Long COVID and related post-viral conditions like ME/CFS remain severely underfunded in Australia compared to countries like the US and the UK. Historically, the Federal Government has spent less than $100,000 a year on ME/CFS research—equivalent to just 42 cents per patient. Because of this historic lack of investment into post-viral syndromes, there are currently no scientifically validated treatments for Long COVID. The burden of care has fallen almost entirely on an already-strained General Practice system, which lacks the practice-based research networks, specific MBS funding, and dedicated long COVID clinical pathways required to support patients. Advocates are desperately calling for the government to prioritise research investment, improve disability support services, and recognise Long COVID as a top public health priority.
